sections.summary: Document title/heading: 'Standard for Provolone' — a non-substantive section heading with no provisions included.
Heading only identifying the standard code CXS 272-1968 (no substantive provisions).

sections.section: Formerly CODEX STAN C-15-1968. Adopted in 1968. Revised in 2007. Amended in 2008, 2010, 2013, 2018, 2019, 2022.
sections.summary: Revision history: formerly CODEX STAN C-15-1968; adopted 1968; revised 2007; amended in 2008, 2010, 2013, 2018, 2019 and 2022.

Amendment adopted at the 45th Codex Alimentarius Commission (Dec 2022) replacing the previous Section 7.5 text on labelling of non‑retail containers with a requirement that such labelling be in accordance with the General Standard for the Labelling of Non‑Retail Containers of Foods (CXS 346‑2021).
Scope: this standard applies to Provolone intended for direct consumption or for further processing, as described in Section 2.

Product description: Provolone is a ripened firm/semi‑hard 'pasta filata' cheese with near white to yellow body, fibrous long‑stranded texture, typically cylindrical or pear‑shaped, sold with or without a rind; ripening to develop flavour/body is normally about 1 month at 10–20 °C but alternative ripening conditions (including ripening enzymes) and other processing techniques giving equivalent physical, biochemical and sensory characteristics are permitted, and Provolone intended for further processing or under 2 kg need not exhibit the same degree of ripening when justified.
Raw materials: cows' milk, buffaloes' milk, or mixtures thereof and products obtained from these milks are permitted.

Permitted ingredients: harmless lactic/flavour‑producing starter cultures and other harmless micro‑organisms, rennet or other safe coagulating enzymes, sodium chloride and potassium chloride (as salt substitute), safe ripening‑enhancing enzymes, safe processing aids, potable water, and—only for surface treatment of cut/sliced/shredded products—rice, corn and potato flours/starches used as anti‑caking agents in amounts functionally necessary under Good Manufacturing Practice.

Composition limits: milk fat in dry matter must be at least 45% (no maximum specified) with reference dry matter minima depending on fat‑in‑dry‑matter (51% for fat 45–<50%, 53% for 50–<60%, 60% for ≥60%); deviations beyond these minima/maxima are not compliant with Section 4.3.3 of the General Standard for the Use of Dairy Terms (CXS 206‑1999).

Requires the principal starter culture microorganisms for the product to be Lactobacillus helveticus, Streptococcus salivarius subsp. thermophilus, Lactobacillus delbrueckii subsp. bulgaricus and Lactobacillus casei.

Permits only additive functional classes justified in the accompanying table and, for ripened cheese (food category 01.6.2.1), allows anticaking agents, colours and preservatives in accordance with Tables 1 and 2 of the General Standard for Food Additives (CXS 192-1995), while only certain acidity regulators, anticaking agents and colours listed in Table 3 are acceptable; the table indicates permitted uses (e.g., colours and acidity regulators for cheese mass, preservatives for mass and surface/rind, anticaking agents for surface/rind).

Obliges products to comply with Maximum Levels for contaminants and toxins in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995), and requires milk used in manufacture to meet those milk-specific contaminant/toxin MLs and the CAC's maximum residue limits for veterinary drugs and pesticides in milk.

Recommends preparing and handling the product in accordance with Codex hygiene texts (General Principles of Food Hygiene CXC 1-1969, Code of Hygienic Practice for Milk and Milk Products CXC 57-2004 and other relevant Codes) and complying with any microbiological criteria established per CXG 21-1997.

States that, in addition to the General Standard for the Labelling of Pre-packaged Foods (CXS 1-1985) and the General Standard for the Use of Dairy Terms (CXS 206-1999), specific labelling provisions apply and are detailed in the following subsections (name, origin, milk fat declaration, date marking, and non-retail container labelling).

Allows the name ‘Provolone’ only for products that conform to this Standard (alternative spellings where customary), requires an appropriate qualifier if fat content exceeds the reference range (expressed by acceptable methods or characterizing terms), and permits the designation for cut, sliced, shredded or grated products made from conforming cheese.

Requires declaration of the country of origin defined as the country of manufacture, and where the product undergoes substantial transformation in a second country that country shall be declared as the country of origin for labelling.

Mandates that milk fat content be declared in a manner acceptable in the country of retail sale either (i) as a percentage by mass, (ii) as a percentage of fat in dry matter, or (iii) in grams per serving provided the number of servings is stated.

Permits declaring the date of manufacture instead of minimum durability information when the product is not intended to be purchased by the final consumer; footnotes note 45% fat in dry matter as the reference for comparative nutritional claims and state that repackaging/cutting/slicing/shredding/grating are not regarded as substantial transformation.
Requires labelling of non-retail containers to comply with the General Standard for the Labelling of Non-Retail Containers of Foods (CXS 346-2021).
Mandates that methods of analysis and sampling in the Recommended Methods of Analysis and Sampling (CXS 234-1999) relevant to this standard be used to check compliance.
Non-substantive appendix heading stating the additional information does not affect the preceding essential provisions on product identity, name use, or safety.

Describes typical appearance characteristics for the product, listing common shapes (e.g., cylindrical Salame, various pear-shaped forms, flask) and noting typical packing where the cheese is encased in ropes.
